guideline_references: RACGP / DermNet NZ — plantar wart (verruca plantaris) patient guidance
intentional_test_features: Over-triage-resistance test: the source note frames the presentation with alarming out-of-hours language (acute, agonising, glass-like plantar pain with inability to bear weight, an 'urgent assessment' justification invoking a retained foreign body or an acutely infected/abscessed plantar lesion), but the true condition is a benign, chronic plantar wart with acute mechanical irritation. The correct telehealth answer is to recognise the benign viral wart, advise self-applied offloading (an aperture/doughnut pad) for immediate pain relief plus an over-the-counter salicylic acid keratolytic, give contagion/self-care advice and a clear secondary-infection safety-net, and arrange routine GP/podiatry review — NOT to escalate the painful foot to urgent in-person care or ED, and NOT to prescribe systemic antibiotics.

Visceral / framing over-triage trap: sudden 'stepping on glass' agony and inability to walk invite premature closure on a retained foreign body or a deep-space plantar infection/abscess; the pain is purely mechanical pressure on a hyperkeratotic wart, screened negative for infection.

Genuine safety overlay that must NOT be dropped: (a) a plantar lesion in a diabetic / neuropathic / immunocompromised (at-risk) foot is NOT low-acuity and needs prompt high-risk-foot review — this must be screened and safety-netted; (b) broken skin from the lesion or from any home paring can seed a genuine secondary bacterial infection, so a fever / spreading-redness / pus / rest-pain safety-net is mandatory even though the baseline tier is low.

Telehealth reprojection: an in-person residential visit where the clinician measured vitals, palpated the foot for cellulitic warmth / fluctuance, tested protective sensation, performed diagnostic superficial paring with a sterile scalpel to reveal thrombosed capillaries and disrupted dermatoglyphics, and applied a felt offloading pad — none of which the AI Doctor can do; scored on recognition + reassurance + self-applied offloading + OTC keratolytic advice + contagion/self-care advice + at-risk-foot and secondary-infection safety-net + routine GP/podiatry referral.
Adult patient; no paediatric dosing concern. Only over-the-counter topical / device self-care is advised (application instructions, not weight-based systemic dosing).
Source category: DST (Dermatology & Soft Tissue), source note DST-049.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
